summary: Drew DeVault opens up about a depressive episode triggered by relentless online harassment directed at him and at projects that use SourceHut. He describes how critics don't merely avoid his tools but actively campaign against anyone who uses them, harassing maintainers and discouraging adoption. He acknowledges past mistakes and expresses genuine regret, but questions whether he will ever be allowed to move past them. The post ends with a plea to redirect any harassment toward him rather than his community, and gratitude for those who have sent supportive messages.
key_points: A SourceHut project maintainer stepped down due to harassment over their platform choice, illustrating that toxicity extends beyond DeVault himself
Critics of Hare and SourceHut go beyond personal preference to actively discourage others from using these tools, treating alternative design choices as immoral
DeVault acknowledges past mistakes and says he has worked to improve, but finds that detractors want permanent exile rather than redemption
The constant harassment is affecting his mental health and motivation to continue working on his projects
He offers himself as a lightning rod, asking harassers to target him rather than other community members
money_quotes: It's not enough to dislike my programming language, but the skeptics must publicly denounce it and discourage others from using it.
It's irresponsible, if not immoral, to design a language without a borrow checker in 2022.
Must I resign myself to my fate for stupid comments I made years ago? I'm sorry, and I've been working to do better. Can I have another chance?
Many of my detractors just want me to shut up. No more blog posts, no new projects. Just go away, Drew.
If you're looking for a strategy to get me to shut up, just ensure that I have a constant flow of toxic comments to read.
I love writing code, at least most of the time. I believe in my principles and I enjoy writing software that embodies them.
And if you must harass anyone over it, at least harass me, and not anyone else. My inbox is at sir@cmpwn.com, and I promise that I will read your email and cry, so that no one else has to.
key_insight: Online harassment in open source doesn't just target controversial individuals — it radiates outward to punish anyone who associates with them, poisoning entire communities.
